01 Introduction - YouTube (2).mp4
Ben Horowitz, who is a well-known CEO, author, and venture capitalist, has maybe one of my favorite quotes about company communication and its importance. He says, 'Perhaps the CEO's most important operational responsibility is designing and implementing the communication architecture for her company. Absent a well-designed communication architecture, information and ideas will stagnate and your company will degenerate into a bad place to work. Now, I love this quote because it places the responsibility of communication exactly where it needs to be. With the company leader, the CEO, you.' While it may sound crazy, at some point, as your company grows, your most important task will be to establish and reinforce how information flows through your organization. This includes everything from the dashboards your teams are reviewing to the meetings they are reviewing them in.
And that is exactly what this session is all about. All about. Now just to recap where we are in this journey together, remember that our mission is to build and install a scalable operating system for your business. To do that, we first needed to document the set of algorithms that form the source code of our company OS. That's why we mapped our company's value journeys, documented our company's playbooks, and assigned— clear roles and accountability— using the high output team canvas. Once we had documented the first version of our set of algorithms, we then moved on to architecting the company's common language, which includes both company scorecards and your company's meeting rhythm, which is our focus today. So our goal for the session is simple.
We want to establish a meeting rhythm so the right information is shared with the right people at the right time, allowing both teams and individuals to make data-driven decisions that lead the company towards its stated goals and objectives. So, how would you describe your company's meetings? O-ring? Interruptive? What about a total waste of time? Well, if you answered anything other than 'our meetings are the most valuable time I spend with my team,' then you're def-in the right place. Look, meetings don't have to be boring and they absolutely shouldn't be a waste of time. And by the time you're finished with this session, you'll have a plan for transforming your meetings into the most valuable, highest leverage time you spend at work. So, let's get started.
